coredata.dc:title: Recent advances and applications of ionic liquids-based photonic materials
coredata.dc:creator: Mahato, Manas
Murakami, Yoichi
Das, Sudhir Kumar
coredata.dc:description: Beyond the traditional applications as electrolytes and solvents, ionic liquids (ILs) have emerged as building blocks or components of various advanced functional photonic materials. ILs as a platform give a chance to modify the physicochemical properties of materials for the intended applications because of their unique properties and structural varieties. These also offer some fascinating features due to the synergetic interaction among the ionic components. Beyond the traditional use of ILs, the active research field has recently triggered several novel photonic materials for versatile advanced optoelectronic applications. In the last few decades, it stimulated advanced applications in photon upconversion, white light generation, bioimaging, sensing, nonlinear optics, etc. An overview of ILs-based photonic materials, including their mechanism, primary material systems, and how they are used in diverse applications with merits and demerits, are also discussed in this review. Some potential directions are also out looked to explore this emerging research topic. We anticipate that this article will enable researchers to quickly access the most current knowledge in the fast-expanding field of ILs-based photonic materials. This will also pave the way for researchers to plan and fabricate new ILs-based photonic materials for versatile applications.

head.ce:title: Recent advances and applications of ionic liquids-based photonic materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Beyond the traditional applications as electrolytes and solvents, ionic liquids (ILs) have emerged as building blocks or components of various advanced functional photonic materials. ILs as a platform give a chance to modify the physicochemical properties of materials for the intended applications because of their unique properties and structural varieties. These also offer some fascinating features due to the synergetic interaction among the ionic components. Beyond the traditional use of ILs, the active research field has recently triggered several novel photonic materials for versatile advanced optoelectronic applications. In the last few decades, it stimulated advanced applications in photon upconversion, white light generation, bioimaging, sensing, nonlinear optics, etc. An overview of ILs-based photonic materials, including their mechanism, primary material systems, and how they are used in diverse applications with merits and demerits, are also discussed in this review. Some potential directions are also out looked to explore this emerging research topic. We anticipate that this article will enable researchers to quickly access the most current knowledge in the fast-expanding field of ILs-based photonic materials. This will also pave the way for researchers to plan and fabricate new ILs-based photonic materials for versatile applications.

Recent advances and applications of ionic liquids-based photonic materials have been reviewed, and its perspectives toward developing advanced functional photonic materials are discussed.
Image, graphical abstract

ce:keyword.ce:text: group of uniform materials based on organic salts

ce:section.ce:para: Ionic liquids (ILs)-based innovative functional materials have received much more attention from the scientific world in the last few decades due to their attractive physicochemical properties and structural diversities that could be fine-tuned based on the desired applications [1–9]. Many research groups have recently developed ILs-based innovative functional materials with designer substrates that have controllable physicochemical properties or with a specific functional group for the intended task, so-called “task-specific ILs (TSILs),” which have tremendous interest and versatile applications beyond the traditional applications of ILs. [10–12] Instead of serving as substitute solvents and electrolytes for organic reactions, catalysis, separation, electrochemistry, and other processes, these materials have evolved into adaptable building blocks for creating cutting-edge functional materials. Recently, ILs have attracted tremendous interest from the scientific communities as the building blocks or components for the design and development of sophisticated functional photonic materials, particularly those containing photoresponsive ionic moieties. Such as, qualities of both ILs and polymers could be imparted into the PILs, where ILs are considered as building blocks that are emerged as brand-new functional materials for developing innovative advanced functional materials. Also, the features of ILs can be incorporated with metallic properties by fusing metal ions in the skeleton of ILs based on the desired applications. On the other hand, in a physical mixture of ILs as a component with composite materials based on the intended application, the nature of ILs is much preserved in the final devices.

A large portion of the use of ILs involves substituting traditional solvents or electrolytes. ILs are changing from solvents and electrolytes to unique functional media, fluids, and materials in a new trending field in the design and development of photonic materials that have recently come to light [10–19]. Notably, integrating ILs with photonic materials has significantly increased their utility in various optoelectronic applications [20] (Fig. 1 ). ILs are extensively addressed in these applications as adaptable and high-potential building blocks to create innovative photonic materials. Sometimes new photonic materials are developed within the skeleton of ILs. The new materials should have significant benefits over conventional molecular materials. Due to the high modularity of ILs, specific functionalization for a desired optoelectronic application could be imparted through the ion-exchange metathesis reaction or by incorporating specific functional groups into the ILs skeleton to form new ion-containing materials. These particular photonic materials have the original materials’ essential characteristics and the inherent qualities of ILs for creating new sophisticated photofunctional materials. However, a significant portion of the review article highlights the use of ILs as solvents, electrolytes or developments of advanced functional materials [2–19]. Literature dealing with the usefulness of ILs in designing and developing photonic materials for various optoelectronic applications highlighting its use in photon upconversion, white light generation, FRET-mediated down conversion, etc., which are rare to date. The present contribution is one of them which demostrates the role of ILs in fabricating various photonic materials for versatile optoelectronic applications.

The present study provides a thorough, in-depth analysis of the prior literature updated with the most recent publications to highlight the most recent developments in ILs assimilated photonic materials and their diverse applications. This analysis gives a crucial understanding of the new opportunities and constraints that could spur the scientific community to adopt IL-integrated photonic materials for their intended applications. In this context, it is essential to note that the area of ILs-based photonic materials is rapidly expanding and creates different possibilities in versatile optoelectronic applications, which has become a hot topic in the scientific communities for future investigation. As a result, it is not easy to explore all of the literature in depth in a single article. Only a few of the most insightful and outstanding articles on each topic have been reviewed; we have attempted to include background information and predictions to provide readers with a general overview that they can take in once at a time. Finally, we offer a summary and point of view on the potential of ILs for designing and creating ILs-based photonic materials for the required photonic applications in each discipline.

One approach to sustainably meet society's expanding energy demand is the effective use of technologies that transform solar energy into various types of secondary energy. However, the efficiencies of such technologies have been restricted by the Shockley-Queisser limit that is almost universally applied to conversions of solar energy using semiconductors [21]; that is, only photons with energies greater than the characteristic energy gap of the material (bandgap for semiconductors and HOMO-LUMO gap for organic systems) could be utilized for the purpose. Therefore, photons with lower energy than these characteristic gaps have been wasted. However, such presently wasted lower-energy photons can be transformed into usable higher-energy photons by performing photon upconversion (UC) [22].

Recently, UC based on TTA between organic molecules (Scheme 1 ) has been intensively investigated [23–28] due to its advantage of applicability to low-intensity and non-coherent sunlight [23], which is expected to increase the efficiencies of broad solar-related technologies. In this UC (TTA-UC), excited-state energy of a molecular triplet state may be transferred from one molecule to another molecule (TET) or fused between two molecules (TTA) as schematically illustrated in Scheme 1, both of which are based on an electron-exchanging mechanism. Since this mechanism, often termed the Dexter mechanism, requires physical contact between the molecules [25,26], fluidic media have often been used to allow for molecular diffusion and efficient energy transfers between the molecules. For this reason, organic solvents have been used in the majority of TTA-UC studies so far. However, their high vapor pressure, low thermal stability (or low safety), and bio-toxicity have prevented such UC liquids from being brought to applications. Especially the high volatility and flammability of organic solvents have been problematic. Such disadvantages associated with the use of organic solvents for TTA-UC can effectively be avoided by using an ILs-based fluidic system, which has been manifested in recent reports [29–40].

In 2011, Murakami, for the first time, reported TTA-UC using ILs [30], where he also presented a theoretical dimensionless solution that describes excitation intensity dependence of UC emission intensity or UC-QY [30,32]. Subsequently, Murakami and co-workers investigated and elucidated the kinetics of TTA-UC in ILs using various transient spectroscopy methods [33,34]. Subsequently, they discovered an important fact that UC-QY increased with the increasing viscosity of IL [35]. These authors explained the mechanism behind this curious discovery in terms of (i) viscosity-dependent rate of intersystem crossing between encounter complexes (denoted j [T1⋅⋅⋅T1]) having different spin multiplicities j (j = 1, 3, or 5) and (ii) viscosity-dependent lifetime of the solvent cage that encases the encounter complex [35]. Regarding mechanism (ii), these authors explained that the higher viscosity should increase the lifetime of the solvent cage and thus interaction time between the paired triplets that have formed an encounter complex j [T1⋅⋅⋅T1], which effectively increases the chance of the intersystem crossing to occur and hence the UC quantum efficiency (UC-QY) [35]. These findings have offered a basic guideline for increasing the UC-QY of TTA-UC in ILs.

However, the use of ILs may cause practical difficulties when they are applied because of a few disadvantages of ILs, which are, for example, lack of biodegradability and high cost. To resolve these issues, Murakami, Das, and co-workers [40] employed DESs, an emerging class of low-cost, highly stable, and environmentally sustainable liquids, for TTA-UC. The use of DESs as fluidic media has revolved around the shortcomings that might be associated with ILs. Eventually, these authors have shown that DESs can be a good replacement for ILs without impairing the kinetics that governs the performance of TTA-UC [40].

The fluidic nature is often undesirable for the practical use of photon upconverters in SCs. For ILs-based upconverters to be used in SCs and other applications, the fluidity of ILs should be suppressed [30,33–35]. To resolve this issue, Murakami and co-workers [36] developed IL-based gelatinized upconverters by using polymer-based ionic salt. In their report, the emitter and sensitizer molecules were stably included in the ionogel, forming translucent and inflammable photon upconverters (Fig. 2 ). Different from intuition, they discovered that gelation actually does not affect the diffusion of solute species. Thus, the fluidity of ILs could successfully be suppressed in the developed ionogels without impairing molecular diffusion, optical transparency, and thermal stability (Fig. 2).

Inspiring by the bicontinuous ionic arrangement of ILs, Yanai and co-workers [37] introduced a TTA-based upconverter using a TTPDPAS chromophoric ILs where DPAS acted as the emitter and PtOEP as the sensitizer (Scheme 2 ). Interestingly, TTPDPAS ILs formed an interconnected array of aromatic chromophores, which acted as efficient channels for triplet energy migration. They revealed a structure-property relationship within TTPDPAS ILs between two-dimensional (2-D) nanostructure and UC efficiency based on TTA [37,38]. It was demonstrated that the asymmetric TTP+ ion formed an ion pair with the chromophore DPAS under ambient circumstances, exhibiting the IC and supercooled ILs phases. From single-crystal X-ray analysis, the IC phase exhibited an alternating alignment of polar (ionic) and non-polar (non-ionic) layers. This multilayer structure endured through the ILs phase. Recent MD simulations also established the structure-property relationship between the 2-D ionic array and UC efficiency [38].

They also developed visible-to-UV UC materials based on non-volatile chromophoric TTPPOS, ILs [39] (Scheme 2). Ir(ppy)3, a triplet sensitizer, was doped in these ILs to perform a visible-to-UV UC with a comparatively low excitation threshold intensity. The relatively large triplet diffusion coefficient (1.4 × 107 cm2s−1) and the high absorption coefficient of 15 cm−1, along with the long triplet-state lifetime of the chromophore in the condensed ILs system, accounted for these properties. These observations suggested that the lifetime of the triplet state played a significant role in this sticky condensed environment [30,31,33–36,40].

Murakami et al. [31] recently achieved a UV-UC to a lower wavelength, less than 340 nm, in several solvents, including ILs. They have found that under continuous photoirradiation, UC-QYs and the photostability of the sensitizer BCA vary depending on the solvent. It was demonstrated that UC-QYs were primarily controlled by the polarity of the solvent, which changes the relative triplet state energy-level between the emitter, DTBN, and sensitizer as a result of the solvatochromic shift imposed on the sensitizer. Notably, Murakami and Kamada [32] recently derived and presented various fundamental functions useful for analyzing and explaining experimental results of TTA-UC in their perspective, which shall be helpful for the scientific communities to design and evaluate new TTA-UC materials.

ILs-based photofunctional materials draw significant attention from scientific communities, especially bearing cations and anions as photoresponsive ones. These particular ILs-based materials display unique PL characteristics that span a substantial area of the visible spectrum. λexc
.-dependent PL behavior is one of the unique characteristics of ILs, particularly those with imidazolium as a cation. In recent years, ILs have been employed to create WLE devices because of their classical λexc
.-dependence emission behavior. Researchers have recently paid great attention to WLE materials because of their critical uses in biological labeling and optoelectronic devices as prospective LEDs that greatly reduce global electricity usage. Recently, WLE materials have been fabricated within the skeleton of ILs, or sometimes ILs are used as a component of light-emitting materials. ILs-mediated production of WLE materials is found to have substantial one in this direction because of their excellent chemical reactivity.

However, in 2012 Patra and colleagues [43] demonstrated that ILs integrating ZnO NCs could generate white light with high efficiency. With a large bandgap (Eg = 3.37 eV at 300 K), ZnO NCs are a notable II-VI semiconductor. The free exciton emission from the broad bandgap is responsible for the high UV luminosity of ZnO NCs in the range from 375 to 405 nm, while the deep-level or trap-state emission is responsible for the green emission peak at 545 nm. By adjusting the reaction conditions, Chen et al. were able to optimize the blue-to-yellow emission of ZnO NCs [44]. Hao et al. created a novel type of ILs crystalline materials containing Zn cations to produce blue and green color fluorescence from ZnO NCs [45]. Patra and coworkers demonstrated an easy method for generating white light from ILs assimilating ZnO NCs, which was found to be novel and unprecedented. This method takes advantage of the unique tailoring emitted nature of ILs in ILs integrated ZnO NCs. A schematic diagram for the various relaxation processes of the photoexcited ILs and ZnO NCs was proposed to comprehend the mechanism of white light generation from the ILs assimilating ZnO NCs [46]. The several energy states of the imidazole moiety of the respective ILs, including the non-bonding state (n), bonding state (π), and antibonding state (π*), are depicted in the left panel of Scheme 4 . Two different types of transition are present in the imidazolium moiety of ILs, namely n-π* and π-π* [47]. The π–π* transition is more noticeable and emits blue emission at about 420 nm because the non-bonding electron is less available. Now, the creation of oxygen vacancies is caused by some O2− ions that have escaped from the host lattice of the ZnO NCs. This oxygen vacancy centre can absorb an electron, leading to the emergence of defect states [48]. There are three energy levels in ZnO NCs, such as defect states, VB, and CB. ZnO NCs are initially photoexcited to produce positive holes in the VB and negative electrons in the CB. The VB hole could trap the oxygen vacancy sites. This surface-trapped hole recombined with an electron after tunneling back into the particle, creating the V0 − centre at the surface. As a result of this centre recombining with an electron in the CB, yellow light may be seen emitting from the system [49]. Consequently, the blue light emitted by the IL's π–π* transition and yellow light produced by the transition between the electron in the CB and the hole trapped in the defect states combined to generate intense white light. The time-resolved decay analysis of the ILs suggests that charge-transfer transition may also be a factor in the generation of white light from the ILs to the ZnO NCs. Kumar and co-workers [50] also fabricated a WLE CdS QDs into the core of an ILs confined domain of a microemulsion compromising HO-EAF, isooctane, and [ProC3]AOT with a quantum efficiency of about 43%.

Jayabharathi et al. [51] subsequently developed very intense WLE materials by utilizing HMIMBF4 ILs to modify the surface of TiO2 NPs. The PL lifetime of TiO2(A) increases when modified within these ILs compared to the bare one. The mechanism of producing white light from these TiO2 NPs integrated ILs are nearly identical to those suggested by Patra and colleagues [43]. TiO2 NPs' defect emission produces intense green and yellow photoluminescence at 517 and 568 nm, respectively. The imidazolium portion of HMIMBF4 ILs undergoes a π–π* transition at a wavelength of about 420 nm, which generates blue emission. The HMIMBF4-TiO2 colloidal hybrid electrolyte produces intense white light due to the interaction between defect emission from TiO2 NPs and blue emission from ILs. The time-resolved decay analysis proved that charge transfer also takes place from HMIMBF4 to TiO2(A). By using HMIMBF4 ILs, the surface of rutile TiO2 was modified to increase device efficiency. The ILs layer experiences spontaneous aligned dipole polarization; it raises device efficiency and decreases the electron injection barrier. Their demonstration suggests a novel design approach and interfacial engineering method to create a novel photonic device for electron carrier injection and transfer that can be helpful in the production of numerous ILs-initiated optoelectronic gadgets.

As a family member of nanoscale materials, QDs have been used in different optoelectronic applications. Recently, Bhandari and co-workers [52] developed multifunctional WLE nano-composites for the first time through the surface modification of an orange light-emitting Mn2+-doped ZnS QDs using blue-emitting choline tosylate ILs which led to the generation of bright white light, with color chromaticity value (0.33, 0.27). The orange light's mutual contribution, which emits from QDs, and blue light radiates from ILs, generate white light. Additionally, the wavelengths of the excitation and the concentrations of ILs affect the emission color and chromaticity color coordinates values. A WLE hydrogel was also prepared by conjugating the WLE QD-ILs composite with an alginate biopolymer which also emits white light. Surprisingly, WLE QD-ILs nanocomposites and WLE hydrogel demonstrate long-term preservation of their structural and PL properties and hold promise for various optoelectronic applications. Further, they extended their concept for fabricating a highly stable WLE QDs nanocomposite compromising orange light emitting Mn2+ doped ZnS QDs, a green light emitting Zn(8-hydroxy quinoline)2 complex with a blue light emitting choline tosylate ILs. WLE nature and structure of QDs preserve for a long time in the solid state of this nanocomposite, expecting its long-term application in versatile photonic devices. [53]

However, there are several disadvantages to the systems covered above, including color purity, reproducibility, long-term stability and laborious preparation techniques, and the high price and scarcity of rare earth elements. In these circumstances, single [54–57] and supra molecular [57] materials have received much attention for producing white light. However, there are small numbers of reports on WLE based on single organic molecular platforms [54–57]. Most of the time, it was revealed that the photostability of such emitters over the visible range was not yet covered. Additionally, few studies on white-light emissions generated from FON [58]. As a result, the development of completely organic nanomaterials to produce WL represents a substantial and almost unsolved issue. Using ILs preparation techniques, Patra and colleagues [59] created a highly photostable organic salt made from red-, green-, and blue-emitting building blocks. Generally, covalent bonds are typically used to connect the species that make up organic light-emitting materials. In this context, Warner and his colleagues generated a ground-breaking research area on low-melting organic salts and coined them as GUMBOS for many applications [58,60–65]. Patra and colleagues [58] extended their approach to creating organic nanoparticles that generate white light for the first time. By employing ILs preparation techniques, the three ion-containing fluorophores, AP (a blue cationic emitter), FL (a green dianion fluorescent), and R6G (a green-to-red spectral band emitting cationic fluorophore), were combined to create the fluorescent organic salt [APR6G][FL] with a relatively low melting point. Nanoparticles derived from [APR6G][FL] trifluorophoric salt using a straightforward reprecipitation approach were found to be approximately 16 nm spherical in size and displayed intense white light with a high quantum efficiency (26%) and color coordinate value (0.29, 0.38) which is close to that of pure white light (0.33, 0.33). Despite the donor-acceptor integrated overlap, efficient white-light generation was achieved. This proposes that parasitic energy transfer may be more prevalent because of "frustrated energy transfer," including GSIPT. Additionally, they revealed that the strong relationship between the dianionic FL2− skeleton and the fluorescent cations AP+ and R6G+, which reduces the band gap has a significant impact on the QY for white-light emission (Scheme 5 ). This special ionic nanoparticle has applications in optoelectronic devices, such as completely organic white (or color-tunable) LEDs with long-lasting stability and reproducible color. Additionally, the application of this technique would help to design and develop different organic ionic fluorophores and their applications ranging from chemo-sensing to biological imaging. These findings also demonstrate that nanoparticles made from an appropriately designed organic salt provide exciting possibilities and flexibility for overcoming typical difficulties associated with current white light generating systems and open up an exciting new avenue for further exploration and exploitation.

However, research using ILs to create WLE materials is still in its early stages. To produce WLE materials with intriguing optoelectronic applications, more focus is needed on designing and developing ILs-based WLE materials based on single organic ions than the ILs-based hybrid nanoscale materials.

Over the few decades, the FRET approach, which has been used to introduce innovative materials for energy down-conversion, has attracted increasing interest. This technique is highly beneficial for developing PL materials to obtain a large shift of the λexc from the native dyes [67,68] in which the PL spectra of the donor should overlap with the absorption spectra of the acceptor. Recently, tremendous attention has been devoted to developing ILs-based PL materials based on FRET for energy down-conversion [69–79].

A unique FRET system using R6G and imidazolium scaffold-based ILs, BMIMCl developed by J. Kadokawa et al. in 2010 [69]. For the first time, they revealed that FRET occurs between R6G, an acceptor, and BMIMCl, a donor, in a solution of R6G in BMIMCl by excitation at a broad wavelength area due to the λexc. -dependent fluorescence behavior of ILs (Scheme 6 ). Additionally, a gel material was created that performs the same functions as a FRET system by employing guar gum to BMIMCl. A special FRET film containing R6G in PILs was subsequently developed, and its FRET behavior was investigated, showing that R6G PL may change depending on the λexc.
[70]. As indicated in Scheme 7 , the two polymeric ILs 1 and 2 were radically copolymerized to create PILs 3. This transparent film exhibits λexc.-dependent PL behavior because the PILs skeleton contains an imidazolium moiety. Then the novel rhodamine derivative 4 was added to the transparent film of PILs 3 by radical ter-polymerization with ILs 1 and 2 (Scheme 7), and a group that could undergo polymerization to generate the film of PIL 5 containing the R6G moieties. The PL resulting from the presence of ILs units 1 and 2 cannot be seen. However, the emission spectra of the film show the PL resulting from the presence of R6G. This finding supports the existence of FRET from units 1 and 2 to the R6G group in the film because all of the emission spectra of the film of 3 excited at different wavelengths partially coincide with an absorption peak of the film of 5 at wavelengths between 450 - 600 nm. Additionally, the small distance is supported by the covalent connections that connect R6G moieties to units 1 and 2 in a polymeric chain, which is likely a factor for the effective FRET process. A tunable multicolor photoluminescent PIL has also been introduced by Kadokawa and co-workers [80] based on FRET incorporating rhodamine, 7-(diethylamino)coumarin-3-carboxylic acid (DEAC), and pyranine moieties by radical copolymerization of polymerizable ILs which emits three primary colors red, green, and blue by the variation of λ exc. By incorporating the possible combinations of the dye moieties in the PIL skeleton, various multicolors emitted PIL films could be fabricated, including white light.

TC-based GUMBOS were prepared by Warner and colleagues [72] using a well-known anion-exchange process. By using the reprecipitation process at micromolar dye concentrations, these TC-based GUMBOS, which have an anion as a hydrophobic one, make it easier to create 1-D nanomaterials at ambient temperature in the micro- and nanometer scales. These nanomaterials display fine-tunable photophysical properties attributed to the efficient FRET process and are quite efficient for energy relays. Subsequently, they [73] introduced some GUMBOS-based dye molecules for down-converted energy relays in dye-sensitized solar cells using an acceptor as a photosensitizing one. Compared to the corresponding parent dyes, some advantageous features have been improved in these GUMBOS. These GUMBOS displayed good spectrum overlap with the photosensitizing dye and demonstrated good energy transfer efficiency between them. The counter-anion variation plays a vital role in enhancing energy transfer efficiency. To create more effective DSSCs, the photophysics of energy relay dyes can be tuned using GUMBOS, which could be fabricated by the straightforward ion-exchange technique.

In 2014, Mandal et al. [74] investigated the excitation energy transfer to learn more about the microscopic environment between the fluorophore and RTILs. It was revealed that the length of the cationic alkyl chain, λexc. and the type of the anions affects the spectral and temporal fluorescence behavior of dissolved solutes in RTILs. Furthermore, the magnitude of RDA depends on the nature of the RTILs. The rise time ( τ
A rise ) and rate constant of energy transfer are not varied with the λexc or the concentration of the acceptor. But, the nature of the cations, more specifically, alkyl chain length on cations and the nature of anions accelerate the magnitude of τ
A rise as well as the rate constant of energy transfer. Subsequently, it was also [75] demonstrated that the FRET mediated down energy conversion between the ILs and cationic acceptor, R6G, where ILs act as donors. The resonance energy transfer between ILs (donor) and R6G dye (acceptor) was also explored by Sarkar and colleagues [76] to understand better the function of ILs' ionic constituents in their structural organization. It has been found that the nature of the cation and length of the anion chain of ILs has a significant impact on the excitation energy transfer process. RDA value increases with increasing the anionic chain length from ethyl to octyl. However, RDA was found to change with the variation of cations. But as the anionic chain length increases, the efficiency of energy transfer efficiency diminishes and is described based on the ionic assemblies of the smaller alkyl group, and the larger is relatively dissimilar. Additionally, it was reported that the natures of the cations of ILs have a major impact on the energy transfer process [75,77,78]. However, energy transfer processes from ILs containing branched and unbranched alkyl chains to R6G were also observed, which was anticipated to be useful in understanding the nano-structural organization of ILs [77]. Additionally, Sarkar et al. [78] further explored the energy transfer process investigations to comprehend how monocationic imidazolium-based ILs and dicationic ILs differ in their microscopic structural organization, even though the alkyl chain length is kept constant. It was found that the energy transfer process is more advantageous for dicationic ILs than monocationic ILs. RDA is higher in monocationic ILs than in dicationic ILs, even though dicationic ILs are slightly bulkier due to adding an extra imidazolium unit to their dicationic one. This finding suggests that the local structure of imidazole in dicationic ILs has a major impact on the energy transfer process. A new type of phosphonium ILs with a pyrene butyrate chromophoric system was recently prepared by Halder et al. [79]. This chromophore creates NPs in an aqueous media with many essential characteristics. Inside the nanomaterial, pyrene units are arranged in a crystalline lattice. In NCs, the fluorophore moieties display ESAIE. When excited, these nanomaterials have a strong propensity for self-association. They could be employed for transferring photonic energy to several hydrophobic dyes, making the system a potential smart material for energy relay applications (Scheme 8 ). Excitation energy transfer between pyrene-modified sulfonate anionic ILs (donor) to fluorescein dodecyl ester (Scheme 9 ) anionic ILs (acceptor) has also been demonstrated recently [71].

Proton is one of the most crucial particles in the universe. The most significant phenomenon associated with it is acidity and basicity. It is an essential phenomenon in nature that living organisms even have a sense of it. The maintenance of acid-base balance is necessary for many biological activities. Additionally, it is also vital for the development of many functional materials, including catalysts [81], sensors [82], imaging agents [83], and proton-conductive membranes [84,85]. The acid-base equilibrium process takes place from aqueous to non-aqueous non-polar solvents. Conventional dyes that can function as colorimetric pH indicators in aqueous solutions could be incorporated within the skeleton of ILs to design & develop ILs-based pH indicators to detect the neutralization point in non-aqueous solvents. With these changes, the sensing moiety becomes more soluble in a broad range of solvents. Eventually, they could be able to analyze non-polar acids and bases quickly and can figure out the acid-base dissociation constants in a wide range of solvents [86].

MR and MO are well-known anionic pH-sensing dyes widely employed in research and development labs and industry. Due to the solubility issue, MR and MO have limited applications. For example, MO is only soluble in aqueous solutions, while MR can only be used in a few non-aqueous environments. For their actual application in various solvents, Deng et al. changed [87] these anion-containing dyes within the skeleton of ILs (Scheme 10 ). ILs-modified MR dye exhibits excellent performance with noticeable color variations in aqueous solutions with different pHs (Fig. 4 ). On the other hand, because of solubility problems, the bare anionic MR does not exhibit noticeable color changes that are visible to the naked eye in aqueous solutions with various pHs. This finding suggests that these anionic dyes modified by ILs can be used to measure the pH of aqueous solutions. Most of the non-aqueous systems do not dissolve MO, which is often used to detect acid-base titration in aqueous solutions. However, most non-aqueous solutions can dissolve or partially dissolve ILs-modified MO dyes. ILs modified MO dye performs acid-dissociated proton sensing better than the unmodified anionic one. However, the colorimetric change and sensing efficacy of ILs-modified MO dyes in various non-aqueous solvents vary significantly, which is dependent on the deprotonation capacity of the corresponding solvents. From this observation, it is clear that the degree of acids dissociation depends on the solvents' nature. Hence acidity is varied from solvent to solvent. Therefore, a new smart indicator and quantitative estimation of the acid dissociation constant (pK a) in a wide choice of solvents is urgently needed to sense the acidic nature of solutions. Also, a correlation of pK a values in various solvents is required to unify the pK a scale. Considering these details, Halder and colleagues [88] designed and created a GUMBOS and nano-GUMBOS-based pH-responsive indicator that attaches MO to the quaternary phosphonium cation. This pH-responsive indicator could sense small concentrations of bases or acids in both polar and non-polar liquids. The overlapping indicator approach was also used to determine the pK a values in non-aqueous solvents. However, Xie and co-workers fabricated a homogeneous, uniformly monolith pH-responsive hybrid organic-inorganic f-PMMA-SiO2 based ionogels containing BMIMMO ILs [89] and employed them for the detection of acid-base colorimetrically.

A fluorogenic RTILs, [TTP+]3[HPTS]3− comprising three non-functional cations, TTP+ in conjunction with photoacid tri-anion HPTS was described by Bright and Baker coworkers [90]. It is a naturally photoluminescent liquid that responds strongly to changes in pH. The fluorescence of [TTP+]3[HPTS]3− varies depending on the analyte. It is interesting to note that, in contrast to free pyranine, [TTP+]3[HPTS]3− exhibits a ratiometric fluorescence sensitivity towards the solution and gas-phase basicity, respectively [91–95]. Based on the analyte-dependent fluorescence responses, a thin film investigation and spectroscopic setup were demonstrated to detect numerous amine vapors (Fig. 5 ). The synthesis, characterization, and pH sensing properties of two new nanomaterials based on RTILs generated from FL anion, and TTP+ cations in various stoichiometries ratios were reported by Warner and coworkers in 2013 [96]. Their research opens up a new path for developing easy nano-sensors formed from a fluorescent dye, where most of the pH-responsive sensory materials are made from the dye. Subsequently, they [97] developed a straightforward, inexpensive, and disposable photonic ILs-based pH, acidic, and basic vapor sensing device. They created 12 ILs using an ion-exchange process combining TTP+ with several anionic pH indicator dyes. These colorimetric ILs-based sensors are used to find and classify chemicals in both aqueous and vapor phases. Various sensor arrays have been created using a widely accessible, inexpensive material as a matrix, which is excellent and compatible with usage in aqueous conditions. Later, Elshaarawy et al. [98] reported a new class of pH sensors and nanosensors based on MeIm-Sal ILs based on the ESIPT mechanism in which photoexcited molecules relax their energy through tautomerization by transfer of protons. ESIPT process displays large Stokes’ shift and dual photoluminescence in which shorter wavelengths originate from the original tautomer and longer-one due to the proton-transferred tautomer. It has been revealed that MeIm-Sal Ils-based new sensor can detect pH fluorescence on a pH scale ranging from 5 to 9. A significant Stokes’ shift, exceptional aqueous solubility, and synchronized color and fluorescence profile alterations are just a few of the advantages of these new fluorescence sensors. Recently, Chen and co-workers [99] reported a [6MQc][Tf2N] ILs for the real-time intercellular pH estimation, which has high biocompatibility, good anti-photobleaching properties, and high tolerance with the change of ionic strength of the solution.

The creation of new ILs-based acid-base sensing materials for the non-aqueous environment and modifications to conventional acid-base indicators are, however, arising challenges and opportunities that may drive researchers to develop ILs-assimilated acid-base indicators and determine their dissociation constants (pK Ind) in any solvents based on their solubility. The pK a values of various acids and bases in aqueous and non-aqueous media can be computed using the pK Ind values. Using this kind of ILs-based indicator without switching out the primary indicator moiety would also be advantageous for determining the pKa scale in various solvents.

Bioimaging technology using synthetic luminescence probes is rapidly expanding among the scientific communities. This technique is expected to recognize and sense the presence of cellular analytes, including biomolecules, metal, and non-metal ions, for the early identification of diseases, which will also accelerate the drug discovery process. The water solubility of synthetic luminescence probes is an essential criterion for their actual application of this technique. TSILs can provide a facile and promising route for designing and developing high luminescence probes with enhanced water solubility. In 2009, Warner and co-workers [61] demonstrated a nano-GUMBOS based on a NIR fluorophore for in vivo fluorescence imaging based on the reprecipitation of HMTAOT GUMBOS (Scheme 12 ). The NIR fluorescent nano-GUMBOS could penetrate in vitro of Vero cells effectively, suggesting its high potential application in biomedical imaging. You and Chen reported in 2010 [103] how to create water-soluble fluorescent bioimaging dyes with strong luminosity by incorporating the imidazolium-based ILs pendants into a squaraine skeleton (Scheme 12). These novel imidazolium-anchored dyes have high water solubility. By adjusting the alkyl chain length connected to the imidazolium unit, it is possible to fine-tune the brightness of these dyes by regulating how they aggregate in aqueous conditions. The imidazolium unit plays a vital role in fulfilling the necessary and sufficient condition of luminescence molecules to act as bioimaging probes. It helps to make squaraine dyes more soluble in water. It is also the driving force for the cellular entry of luminescence probes. Again, this is due to the positive charges repelling one another electrostatically, which could decrease the propensity for dye aggregation. The amphiphilic property of this ILs-modified dye increases the cell permeability for its effective use as a bioimaging probe. Most crucially, the alkyl chain length connected to the imidazolium unit can be properly adjusted to control the amphiphilicity and aggregation behavior of squaraine dyes.

Fluorescent CDs have shown promising bioimaging and medication delivery results due to their easy surface-functionalization, non-toxic, and high photostability properties [104–106]. The majority of CDs are hydrophilic reported so far. Nevertheless, well-soluble OCDs and HCDs are particularly curious about their applicability in hydrophobic environments such as cell membranes. Many anti-cancer medications have hydrophobic drug delivery properties. To increase the pace of drug loading, OCDs are a viable and unique alternative to other nanomedicine carriers [107–109]. Even though studies on HCDs are significantly more numerous than OCDs, it makes cell membrane penetration into the body faster, assuring great drug delivery efficiency. Some of the synthetic routes have been reported so far to prepare OCDs. Hydrophobic long-chain organic molecules, such as dodecanethiol, hexadecyl amine, and poloxamer as surface passivation/capping agents, are primarily used to fabric OCDs [110–112]. Another approach is altering the HCDs surface with hydrophobic moiety to tune/regulate the CDs' hydrophobicity [113]. The diverse and changeable nature of the ionic moiety of ILs makes it feasible to precisely adjust/handle its characters. For the creation of ILs-mediated CDs, this property of ILs has been used as a carbon source. The biocompatibility of CDs after ILs modification is greatly enhanced [114]. IL-OCDs use multiple paths to cross the cell membrane, allowing for quick entry into the inside of the cell [115]. Due to their appealing combination of characteristics, this includes their small size, brilliant brightness, and benign nature, ILs-modified CDs offer significant promise in bioimaging applications. Using HeLa cells as a model, Jin et al. [116] examined the utility of ILs-modified CDs in biological imaging in 2014. Cells with internal ILs-assimilated CDs are found to be brightly illuminated. ILs-assimilated CDs are mostly located in cell membranes and cytoplasm, as revealed by the bright luminescence of ILs-based CDs. However, the nucleus region's luminosity is weak, suggesting that IL-CDs are unable to damage the genetic material. These findings demonstrate that living cells are affected by these unique NPs without hampering the decomposition of gene passage [117–121]. These outcomes revealed the IL-CDs' excellent biocompatibility, making them conceivable to be applied for cell imaging.

Subsequently, Chen and Wang et al. [122] in 2017 reported ILs-mediated OCDs for drug delivery and bioimaging applications. For this purpose, they have chosen 11 imidazolium scaffolds negotiated ILs to evaluate their effect on IL-CDs' properties. Fig. 8 depicts the fabrication of ILs-based HCDs and ILs-based OCDs. They have divided 11 ILs into two categories; the first group contains 5 ILs with the same anion BF4 − and the different side chains of cations (EMIMBF4, BMIMBF4, HMIMBF4, OMIMBF4, DMIMBF4). The second portion has 6 ILs with the same cation BBIM and various anions (BBIMCl, BBIMBr, BBIMBF4, BBIMPF6, BMIMNO3, and BBIMTf2N).

Fabricated ILs-based HCDs and OCDs have been utilized for bioimaging applications with a model of HeLa cells at a concentration level of 20 µg mL−1. ILs-based HCDs have exhibited a much lower capacity for cell (bio) imaging than ILs-based OCDs. The luminosity incubated with BBIMNO3-HCDs is 4-fold lower than that of cultured HeLa cell BBIMNO3-OCDs with excitation at 495 nm. According to Overton's rule [123], penetration power into the cell interior of BBIMNO3-OCDs is much more comfortable than that for BBIMNO3-HCDs. Although BBIMNO3-OCDs QYs are lower than BBIMNO3-HCD QYs. However, the quick uptake and storage of ILs-based OCDs inside the cells result in a significantly improved capacity for cell imaging. Fig. 9 demonstrates how cell imaging is dependent on the structure of ILs-OCDs. When the side chain's length in the cation of ILs-OCDs, e.g., EMIMBF4-OCDs, BMIMBF4-OCDs, HMIMBF4-OCDs, OMIMBF4-OCDs have increased, the capacity of cell imaging increases. Fig. 9 shows that, despite having the highest QYs value, HeLa cells cultured with DMIMBF4-OCDs are the darkest, which is likely because ILs-OCDs were endocytosed and interacted with the organelles. By lengthening the imidazolium cation's side chain and lowering the nucleophilicity of its anionic counterparts, it is possible to increase the QYs of ILs-OCDs quickly.

Using an eco-friendly (green) strategy based on ILs, Koh, and Lee et al. [124] reported a way to recycle cellulose waste papers in 2018. CDs have been created from the regenerated cellulose, and RC-CDs are a potential fluorescent probe for bioimaging that exhibits lesser cytotoxicity (Fig. 10 ). As illustrated in Fig. 10, they have employed an innovative, viable, and promising method to produce CDs from waste papers by utilizing ILs environmentally friendly manner. The microwave-assisted pyrolytic technique and recycled cellulose-based CDs (RC-CDs) have successfully rejuvenated the waste paper and transformed it into a value-added product. These RC-CDs have also been employed as effective fluorescent bioimaging probes.

In bioanalytical chemistry, comprehending the appropriation and focus on different metal ions is a crucial area of interest with applications in enzyme catalysis, drug development, and cell signaling. As a result, detecting metal ions and their extraction represent important analytical challenges for biologists and chemists. Metal ion sensors based on ILs have recently become popular and vital to extraction and sensing performance. Mercury and other heavy metal ions (HMIs) contribute to unfavorable natural and medical conditions. Mercury is regarded as the most dangerous non-radioactive metal. Mercuric ion (Hg2+), among the different structures (including the harmful organic derivatives methylmercury), is particularly water-soluble and dangerous, making it bioavailable to humans and other living organisms through the consumption of water. However, most of the sensors either have limitations on their sensitivity [125] or are not utilized for useful applications like identifying and removing Hg2+ from water sources. In 2010, Marchand and Vaultier co-workers [126] introduced TSILs compromising with the 8-hydroxyquinoline benzoate-based binding unit for Hg2+ ions and hydrophobic Tf2N as a counter anion (Scheme 13 ). Due to the hydrophobicity of Tf2N, it is possible to extract the HMIs from the aqueous solutions. The detection and extraction of HMIs are based on the mechanism that, upon binding with TSILs, Hg2+ ions diffused from a flowing transporter aqueous solution to a storage ILs phase. Hence, the luminosity of TSILs exclusively enhances upon binding with Hg2+. Due to the extremely narrow offset signal, such an "off-on" sensing response to metal-ion binding reduces the chance of false-positive results, which is anticipated to increase the detection limit.

Although many fluoroionphores have been created to detect HMIs, very few exhibit a limit of detection below the maximum levels of contamination in consuming water, and very few could detect and eliminate HMIs. By incorporating a rhodaminethiospirolactam as a fluoroionophore within the skeleton of ILs, Zhang and Tan et al. [127] created a new fluoroionophore ILs-based hybrid technique in 2012 to improve the performance of traditional fluoroionophores through a synergistic extraction effect and achieve simultaneous instrument-free recognition and elimination of HMIs, particularly Hg2+ (Scheme 13). They have demonstrated that such a method could afford target improvement to expand the recognition sensitivity of fluoroionophore-based TSILs for target HMIs via the synergistic extraction effect (Fig. 11 ).

In 2015, Paital et al. [128] published the first report on a group of novel fluorogenic hydrophobic TSILs based on a tetradentate ligand derived from naphthalene (Scheme 13) that is highly selective for binding copper (II) and may serve as recyclable extractants for copper (II) with high reversibility (Fig. 12 ). Further, they have developed a new conceptual sustainable approach to enable dual applications of TSILs for detecting and extracting toxic metal ions through an anion exchange mechanism. The hydrophilic ILs bearing hydrophilic anions are used for fluorescence sensing metal ions in the pure aqueous system and living marine organisms. Corresponding hydrophobic ILs bearing hydrophilic anions are employed in extraction processes that could be recycled through metal-ion binding [129].

An EMIMTC-coated gold nanoparticle (IL-Au NPs) was introduced by Jiang et al. [130], having a promising application for the selective detection of Al3+ ions and employed for the determination of Al3+ ions in vermicelli samples. Two eco-friendly and hydrostable novel luminous lanthanide-doped RTILs referred to as [BMIM][Ln(NO3)4] (Ln = Dy, Sm) were reported by Guan et al. in 2016 [131] and may be used to detect Fe(III) ions without the involvement of other metal ions. These ILs demonstrated strong luminous properties and are suitable for usage as soft luminescent materials. These Dy and Sm doped integrated ILs could detect Fe (III) with a remarkable color change and visualize with the naked eye under 365 nm UV light irradiation. This report offers a unique, simple synthetic route for designing and creating ILs doped with lanthanides for use in fluorescence sensors. A TSILs-enabled DNAzyme-based mercury sensor was introduced by Lou and co-workers to detect mercury selectively in solid food samples where ILs are used in a matrix and have 100 times more sensitivity than the non–ILs one [132]. A lipophilic colorimetric calcium-sensitive ILs was developed by Hisamoto and coworkers [133] in 2019 by the combination of cationic dye, [KD-M13][OP2P] (KD-M13: a merocyanine dye and OP2P: bis(4-n-octyl phenyl) phosphate) (Scheme 13). By protonation, these new ILs create a monocation. Although the raw material for these ILs is normally solid, it is fascinating to note that the ion pair form fabricated from these raw materials is liquid at room temperature. The ion-exchange equilibrium of protons and cations in an aqueous solution and at the PVC membrane interface might be thought of as the binding mechanism for Ca2+. This ILs integrated sensor exhibited dramatically promoted sensitivity 13 times more than that of a standard sensor which is completely reversible and selective to Ca2+. Subsequently, they [134] also demonstrated a nanohybrid material composed of [KD-M13][OP2P] ILs-based colorimetric membrane and a titanium dioxide (TiO2)-based 2D photonic crystal slab (CM/PCS hybrid) with the help of nanoprinting and spin coating method. These CM/PCS nanohybrid materials show drastic improvement in the Ca2+ ion sensing performance, ca. 78 times higher than the earlier one. Their demonstration is expected to be applicable in the bioanalytical analysis of Ca2+ ion detection. A new class of unique fluorescent ILs having HQS as an anion, [TTP][HQS], (Scheme 13) for the sensitive detection of Al3+ ion through the inhibition of ESIPT mechanism is recently developed by Wang et al. [135] (Fig. 13 ). Due to its improved sensitivity and selectivity, [TTP][HQS] has a higher binding affinity to the Al3+ metal ion than the conventional probe HQS.

Recently, Ongun et al. [136] employed BMIMSCN ILs for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions selectively over the other ions for the first time, and a thin film was fabricated for on-site detection. A solid-state fluorescent sensor based on CD obtained from photopolymerization of N-VP, BA, and 1,4-Butanediyl-3,3′ -bis-1-vinyl imidazolium DVIM was introduced by Corrales et al. [137] to detect Fe3+ ions in the aqueous medium selectively by PL quenching methods. An N-doped graphitic PL carbon dots with high QY having good selectivity and sensitivity toward Fe3+ ions obtained from cellulose via solution synthesis using BMIMOAc ILs was reported by Kim and co-workers [138], and a CD-embedded cellulose film having selective sensitivity and reusability has been fabricated for its real application (Fig. 14 ). Further, photoluminescence imidazolium ILs based on POSS were introduced by Feng et al. [139], having selective sensitivity toward Fe3+ ions and opening a new avenue to explore more POSS-based practical sensors in the future.

An emerging area of research in sensing chemistry is the design and development of ILs-based anion sensors, albeit this matter is still fresh. Due to its non-volatility and thermal stability, this IL-based anion sensor has several exceptional benefits over a similar non-IL-based one. In 2008, Li and coworkers [140] demonstrated a novel anionic signaling strategy for the "naked-eye" detection of various anions based on the fine-tuning reversibility of hydrogel and porous structure of a PILs-based photonic crystal by exchanging counter anions (Fig. 15 ). Subsequently, a dye ionogel based on RTILs containing BMIMMO and PMMA was reported by Toubert et al. in 2014, which shows strong sensing performance towards protons and hydroxide ions in aqueous to the non-aqueous media, and sensing performance is found to be completely reversible [102]. Further, BMIMMR has been employed to detect trace amounts of nitrite in supplies of water, meat, and dairy products colorimetrically [141]. However, an anion optode based on plasticized PVC membrane containing TTP+ cation and naphtholphthalein dianion has been reported by Hisamoto and coworkers [142] for the selective detection and extraction of anions under the acidic and basic conditions from the aqueous solution. Subsequently, they have also introduced anion selective PVC membrane based on the FRET process composed of hydrophobic TTP+ cation and pyrene modified with sulfonate anion where pyrene acts as a donor and fluorescein dodecyl ester anion as an acceptor [71]. For the selective ion extraction process, the donor and acceptor are long-lasting in a PVC membrane with negligible dye molecules percolation under acidic and basic conditions [71]. Further, they introduced chloride (Cl−) selective coumarin dye liquid nanoemulsion sensors with high selectivity and sensitivity [143]. Recently, BMIMPF6 ILs-based graphene QDs nanocomposite containing 1,3,6-trinitropyrene as an optical signaling unit was fabricated and utilized for the selective detection of sulfide ions (S2−) selectively having a detection limit of 23 nM in an aqueous medium based on PL quenching mechanism [144].

The cyanide (CN−) ion is the most dangerous anion for humans compared to other anions. CN− ions containing chemicals are widely employed in producing synthetic textiles, herbicides, and gold extraction techniques [145]. However, CN− ion is the "murder poison" and is incredibly poisonous. Hence, onsite colorimetric detection of CN− ions is fundamentally important without any instrumental help. A novel design technique was employed by Zhuge et al. [146] in 2019 to create an IL-modified photochromic diarylethenes-based technology for creating a colorimetric anion sensor to identify CN− (Fig. 16 ). It is demonstrated that this IL-modified photochromic diarylethene could identify CN− ion by the naked eye through colorimetric detection with a low-level detection limit.

Recently, novel generations of luminous ILs have emerged to design and develop various advanced functional materials for versatile optoelectronic applications. The photo-responsive matching of ionic moieties with a specific task with unique physical, chemical, or biological properties could be produced for the desired applications [147–159]. Intrinsically luminous ILs, in which cations and anions are typically photoluminescent by nature, have also been created within this framework. This technique represents a new tactic for creating photochemically active advanced functional materials. Usually, for obtaining high luminescent materials based on ILs, the photochromic [160,161] or photoluminescent compound [162,163] is used to dissolve in neat ILs. However, several groups have outlined the potential pairing of particular molecules, such as metal complexes [164–170] or photoluminescent compounds [155], with the appropriate pairing of ionic species of ILs to generate intrinsically luminescent ILs (Scheme 14 ) in the form of unique technological molecular liquids [171]. These ILs can be extremely useful in various fields, including solar cells, fluorescence sensors, and foldable light-emitting devices, avoiding highly volatile organic solvents and enabling strong interactions between the luminescence moieties due to their high concentrations [166].

However, Sarkar and co-workers [192] reported the covalently linked anthracene-based monocationic and dicationic imidazolium ILs with their photophysics and biological application. The pyrene-1-sulfonate (PS) anion approach to producing novel luminous ILs was subsequently described by Castriciano et al. in 2018 [193]. These new ILs were synthesized via simple ion-exchange reactions with the sodium salt of PS and suitable halide cation precursors. The synthesis, thermal characteristics, and photochromic capabilities of ILs, including cation-containing spiropyrans [Cn-SP]X (Cn-SP=1-alkyl-3,3,5′,6′-tetrametylspiro[indoline-2,2′-[2H]pyran[3,2 b]pyridinium], n=8, 4, and 1) were reported by Funasako et al. in 2019. [194] It was demonstrated that spiropyrans exhibit fast and drastic color changes upon photoirradiation. In salts' crystal structures, short alkyl chains lead to the salts' liquefaction, which is crucial to photochromism. A curcumin-based ILs were introduced by He et al. and employed [195] as a photoluminescence and colorimetric probe for the specific detection of benzoyl peroxide selectively over the other peroxide. An AIE, ionic liquid crystals (ILCs) based on diphenylacrylonitrile-imidazole salts bearing long alkyl chain length with various anions were reported by Yang et al. [196] having excellent cyan photoluminescence in the aggregated state. A series of new fluorescent ILs based on isoquinolino [2,1-a] quinoxalin-5-ium was introduced by Yin and Li in 2020 [197], having dual or triple emissions with huge stokes’ shift and mechanochromic behaviors which could be utilized for pH and polarity estimation of the solvents.

One of the most critical gaseous materials on the earth is CO2. Living beings produce it, and at higher concentrations, it is hazardous for living beings and the environment. There are numerous uses for knowing the CO2 content, including environmental monitoring [198]. Its many uses include detecting gas levels in greenhouses (when the amount of CO2 is increased to 1% to improve plant growth and get rid of pests) and measuring CO2 levels in blood [199,200], saltwater [201–203], respiratory analyses [204], and seashells, among others. So far, most of the optical sensors developed for CO2 detection have stopped working at higher percentages of CO2 [202,204–208]. Hence, the development of sensitive sensors for this range is exciting. However, the design and development of fluorescent sensors for CO2 detection is not an easy task within this sensitivity. Few photoluminescence pH indicators meet the conditions of photostability and their accompanying pK a values due to the rigidity of luminous sensors. The optical CO2 sensors described so far can be divided into two groups: based on the (i) Severinghaus technique [205] and (ii) plastic films [206]. For the first type, a gas-permeable membrane separates the studied gas or liquid medium, which takes place in a bicarbonate buffer medium (where a pH indicator is dissolved) [201,207,208]. Although less susceptible to very acidic gases and more stable over time, such sensors are difficult to make. Compared to sensors built using the Severinghaus process, manufacturing plastic solid-state sensor films [209] is more comfortable. A hydrophobic polymer dissolves a lipophilic organic base and a pH indicator [202,206,210–213]. The sensitivity of such materials is not less for application in food packaging technology. However, emulsion-based materials for optical sensing of ammonia [214] and CO2 [215] were reported depending on the aqueous systems of indicators. Such systems, however, are not easily repeatable since the sensor finds it challenging to regain its initial state after dehydration. To circumvent the aforementioned problem, several ILs-based optical sensors have been introduced, which has been elaborated nicely in a recently published review article by Pandey and co-workers [216]. Considering the advantages of ILs, a fluorosensor based on ILs containing HPTS has been demonstrated [217] by Ertekin and co-workers. They also introduced ILs containing BTB-based colorimetric CO2 sensors without loss of its efficiency [218]. Subsequently, Wolfbeis and co-workers fabricated a novel CO2 sensory material (SM) based on an emulsion of an RTIL in a silicone matrix [219,220]. Due to their exceptional thermal stability and minimal vapor pressure, RTILs can be a great substitute for an aqueous emulsion. RTILs possess excellent solubility for CO2 [221–224], making them particularly attractive for this gas sensing. They fabricated several CO2 SM containing different ILs and pH indicators [BMIMBF4-HPTS-NaHCO3 (SM-1), BMIMBF4-HPTS-Na3PO4 (SM-2), BMIMtosylate-HPTS-NaHCO3 (SM-3), BMIMBF4-TB-TBOH (SM-4) and BMIMBF4-BTB-TBOH (SM-5)]

An indicator in the RTIL-based system is almost the same as in the aqueous buffer solution. Different base components were used in the buffer system for tuning the material sensitivity. SM based on BMIMBF4 showed a significantly lower response to CO2 than the material used for tosylate salts of BMIM. CO2 dissolved more readily in ILs made of a larger anion such as tosylate and was found to be more sensitive. The sensitivity of the cation for CO2 detection increased with the length of the alkyl chain. However, optical CO2 sensors' sensitivity was observed to be strongly temperature-dependent [225–227]. To appropriately compute CO2 concentration, the temperature should be established in an independent measurement because it can fluctuate in many actual applications. Therefore, Borisov et al. [226] looked at how well CO2-sensitive materials could sense temperature changes. It can be noticed that the BMIMBF4-based sensor materials did not exhibit consistent performance when tweaking temperatures. The fluorescence intensity of the SM-1 and SM-2 sensor materials varied simultaneously in the absence of CO2, which is highest within 20–30°C. The performance of the sensor materials SM-1 and SM-2 is degraded at higher and lower temperatures, even though they function consistently at ambient temperature might be due to the indicator and base's poor solubility in the RTIL at both low and high temperatures. Therefore, the sensor materials SM-1 and SM-2 are not well suited for real-world applications, such as food packaging technology, as the difference in room temperature and sensor storage at different temperatures will reduce reproducibility. The sensor tosylate salts of SM-3 exhibits entirely reproducible behavior at temperatures between 10 to 50°C, in contrast to the materials based on BMIMBF4. As the temperature rises, the sensitivity drops. Both plastic and all-optical CO2 sensors exhibit this kind of behavior frequently. Tosylate ions' polar sulfonate groups have favored the ionic species' solubility, and their aromatic nature may also aid in the solubilization of HPTS. Additionally, it has fully reversible sensing capabilities for CO2 detection. The response times are determined to be 50 sec when the CO2 level is increased from 0 to 100% and 320 s when it is decreased.

While NR, PR, CR, m-CP, and colorimetric pH indicators could also be theoretically beneficial, they are disregarded due to their unfavorable spectral characteristics. The pK a of an indicator employed determines the sensitivity of the system. The sensor built on BTB, having less pK a value, is responsive to CO2 with a color change from dark blue to yellowish-green in the range of 0 to 100% and could be appropriate for food packaging technology with substantially higher sensitivity. Even in the absence of CO2, the indicator's high pK a value makes it unlikely that it will completely deprotonate. It could be seen visually in the dynamic range of 0 to 2% CO2, with higher analyte concentrations rendering the sensor unresponsive. However, in enclosed spaces where CO2 is used or stored, as well as in enclosed, poorly ventilated work locations, far more sensitive sensors may be appropriate for CO2 monitoring.

A photoluminescence carbazole-based derivative dissolved in EMIMBF4 was also introduced to detect and quantify the dissolved CO2 without extra protection from the ambient air of the lab [228]. A CO2 reaction-based ILs formation sensor has also been developed employing an AIE-based TPE molecule which displays colorimetric photoluminescence change visible to the naked eye attributed to the enhancement of viscosity of the medium due to the formation of ILs due to the reaction between DBU and CO2 [229]. A CO2 reaction-based scheme introducing CIL has been applied for the quantification of dissolved CO2 over the wide concentration range employing HPS, an AIE-based fluorophore, by tuning the polarity and viscosity of the medium [230]. Subsequently, the reaction-based CIL formation was also introduced by Baker co-workers [231] as an optical CO2 sensor based on the excimer photoluminescence of BPP. Further, a ratiometric benzobisimidazolium-based chemosensor has also been developed by Yoon co-workers [232] that works in the absence of an exogenous base ascribed to the formation of N-heterocyclic carbene intermediate that undergoes an imidazolium carboxylate formation upon reaction with CO2. Also, a turn-off-on fluorosensor has been demonstrated by Lee and co-workers recently based on an amine-functionalized imidazolium PIL that works in the presence of TEA. TEA deprotonates the C2-H proton of the imidazolium moiety, and the photoluminescence of PIL was found to be quenched. Subsequently, carboxylation occurred in the presence of CO2 and regained its photoluminescence again. A PIL-based µ-printed optical fiber-tip-based sensor was also developed by Zhang and co-workers for the in-situ detection of CO2 [233]. A 2-(2’-phenoxide) benzoxazole anion functionalized fluorescence ILs (FILs) was also introduced by Wang et al. through the anion exchange metathesis reaction with TPP cation having high sensing performance to detect SO2 gas selectively and reversibly through colorimetric and fluorescence quenching methods [234]. Further, they reported an 8-hydroxyquinoline containing FILs bearing TTP as a cation for detecting and monitoring CO2 by fluorescence quenching [235].

A fundamental study of nonlinear optics is the investigation of light-matter interactions in nonlinear media where dielectric polarization responds nonlinearly to the electric field of electromagnetic radiation with higher intensity. Organic NLO materials have many applications in information technology and industry. These have several advantages compared to inorganic NLO materials. Compared to inorganic NLO materials, organic NLO materials have a faster response time, a lower dielectric constant, and greater synthetic flexibility [236–241]. However, these have poor processability and low thermal stability [242,243]. Modifying these materials within the building blocks of ILs is one of the best ways to overcome these disadvantages of organic NLO materials.

The nonlinear index coefficient (n2) and linear absorption coefficient (α2) are used to measure the third-order NLO features of typical ILs [244–247]. In 2003, Sesto et al. [244] examined the NLO characteristics of various ILs. They synthesized several TTP-based ILs and paired them with different anions, such as NDA, DTMN, MX, and DDTC. It demonstrated that these ILs exhibit significant third-order NLO behavior. For instance, TTPDTMN, which has a dianion with more sulfur atoms, showed the greatest n2, around 20,000 times more than that of CS2 and ten times larger than that of TTPDDTC [244]. The Z-scan method [245–247] explored the NLO behavior of some imidazolium ILs with varying alkyl chain lengths and various anions, including BF4, PF6, NTf2, and CF3COO−. All these ILs display a modest value of α2 but a substantial anion-dependent n2 [245]. EMIMCF3COO− demonstrates the largest n2 under a continuous-wave argon laser at 514 nm] [246].

Studies were also done on the second-order NLO properties of ILs and the third-order NLO properties. Since substances with second-order NLO activity must be noncentrosymmetric at the molecular and material levels, Xiao et al. [248] created a series of ILs with imidazolium cations conjugated with aromatic cores with the variation of donor-acceptor substituents and anions (Scheme 16 ). They found that both anions and substituents influence the second-order NLO characteristics differently. More specifically, shifting the anion from PF6 to NTf2 decreases the initial hyperpolarizability (b), whereas changing the substituent from hydrogen to an electron-donating hydroxyl or methoxyl group improves the value of b for a given substituent on the phenyl ring. These findings suggest that ionic salts with a particular second-order NLO response might have more hyperpolarizability because the substituent on the phenyl ring has a stronger propensity to donate electrons.

Subsequently, RF Souza et al. [245] investigated the nonlinear properties of two ILs, BMIMPF6, and BMIMBF4, using nonlinear refraction/absorption and thermo-optical measurements using two different laser wavelengths, 514 nm and 810 nm, in the continuous wave and femtosecond regimes, respectively. However, two distinct n2 values were revealed by the Z-scan method at 810 nm, and these were observed in femtosecond laser excitation for a high repetition rate of about 76 MHz. The measured nonlinear refractive indices were determined to be n2 = -1.8 × 10−9 cm2 W−1 and n2 = -2.7 × 10−9 cm2 W−1, for BMIMBF4 and BMIM PF6 respectively. Large negative nonlinear thermo-optical coefficients and refractive indexes with negligible absorption were observed for these ILs. Andrade-Lucio and his group [246] characterized the NLO properties of several ILs, namely, EMIMTF2N, BMIMBF4, EMIMCF3COO, and BMIMCF3COO, respectively, and the source of nonlinear refractive index is mainly thermal. It was found that the NLO characteristics seen in these ILs are stabilized by the CF3COO− anion. It was demonstrated that EMIMCF3COO represents the utmost nonlinear refractive index and optical limiters which is beneficial for optoelectronics device design and makes it the guide for nonlocal applications waves. Santos and his team [247] looked into how the anionic and cationic components affect the nonlocal nonlinearity of ILs. They considered PF6, BF4, and Tf2N as the anionic components with imidazolium derivatives with the variation of alkyl chain length (CnMI, with n = 4, 6, 8, 10, and 12). It was found that BMIMBF4 has the highest modulus value, while the HMIMTf2N and DMIMTf2N have lower values. Additionally, it was noted that the ILs' anionic component, Tf2N, has lower values than BF4 and PF6. However, alteration of the cationic component does not significantly alter the IL's nonlocal response. Additionally, by employing violet and infrared excitation, they observed that the n2 values are roughly two orders of magnitude higher for the shorter light wavelength. However, Nóvoa-López et al. [249] investigated the NLO behavior of BMIM cation with anions containing transition metals such as Zn, Cu, Ni, or Co by the open Z-apparatus method. They used an 80-fs pulse, λexc =810 nm, and the Ti: Sapphire oscillator laser source repetition rate to conduct the investigation. Four ILs with metallic atoms have shown nonlinearity under these experimental conditions. Alencar groups [250] reported the 3rd-order non-linearity properties of the system having colloidal Ag-nanoparticle in BMIMBF4. They measured the filling factors of the colloids with different nanoparticles along with nonlinear refractive indexes of BMIMBF4 using a thermally managed Z-scan. Singh and his group [251] revealed the effects of –OH functionalization, C2 methylation, and the influence of an excess radiation field on the non-linear optical response on an imidazolium-based RTIL by the femtosecond Z-scan method. They observed the large negative nonlinear refractive index values of n2 for all RTIL, mainly due to the thermal effect. Further, such a decrease in n2 values leads to a decrease in the non-linearity of the –OH function and C2 methylation of the methyl cation. Thus, the charge transfer between the anion and the cationic species through the C2 methylation has been greatly affected due to such decreases in the non-linearity values. These interns are weakening the ion-pair formation. Further, they reported that the high irradiation leads to increase n2 values, which form the radiolytic product that consists of a double bond unit and possesses a strong interionic interaction. Moreover, they also observed similarities between n2 values for both high and low pulse repetition rates. Castellanos-Águila et al. [252] reported the retention of the geometric symmetry of pyridinium and imidazolium cation containing ILs with the variation of alkyl chain length. Although long alkyl chains on cations have greater hyperpolarizabilities which depend on the shape and size of the individual cations. The ICT between the pyridinium or imidazolium rings may help to explain why these cations have hyperpolarizability. Further, they explored the NLO characteristics of the [alkylPy]+[NO3]− and [alkylMIM]+[NO3]− ILs by evaluating initial hyperpolarizability, their dipole moment, polarizability, and anisotropy of polarizability. As the alkyl chain length increases, both systems have amplified the polarizability and the initial hyperpolarizability. This can be likely because polarizability strongly depends on the interactions between the ions, which are directly proportional to the intermolecular distances. Furthermore, they revealed the energy gap, and first, hyperpolarizability presents a converse relationship for all studied ILs. NLO properties of several symmetrical ILs was also evaluated by Vázquez's co-workers [253] employing the Z-scan technique. It was found that nonlinear refractive index and absorption coefficient are because of high concentrations having symmetrical structures and non-aliphatic chains. Subsequently, the NLO behavior of some asymmetrical ILs was also investigated with the variation of several anions by the Z-scan method [254]. Among the anions, NO3 − containing ILs have the best nonlinear values having higher polarizability, and the length of the alkyl chain on cationic moiety profoundly enhances the nonlinear behavior. Anions' dependence on the NLO behavior of ILs was studied by Bardak et al. [255] employing DFT analysis. It was revealed that anions having highly delocalized electrons are anticipated to produce rich NLO characteristics, and ILs with high electron density have strong hyperpolarizabilities. Effects of aromatic and non-aromatic ILs, e.g., imidazolium and DABCO–based on the third-order NLO response of porphyrin moieties and its Co-complex were reported by Sabbaghan et al. [256] by the Z-scan technique, and a positive synergetic effect was revealed due to the addition of imidazolium-based ILs.

The NLO characteristics of four ILs, BMIMHSO4, BIMHSO4, MIMHSO4, and BMIMCF3SO3 was recently explored theoretically and practically by Ferreira et al. [257]. Their findings demonstrate a coherent relationship between the growth of nonlinear values and the refractive index, as revealed by the Z-scan technique. Further, second-order NLO properties of several ILs bearing various cationic and anionic moieties were revealed by DFT analysis, and it found that local interactions between the ions are the key factor in determining NLO behavior [258]. The effect of electronic charge delocalization on molecular polarizabilities of ILs was also demonstrated by Varela and co-workers [259] through DFT analysis. They revealed that highly conjugated cations could be used together with anions to design and develop ILs-based NLO materials with very high refractive indices. However, charge delocalization and geometry of the corresponding ILs are the key factors governing hyperpolarizabilities. High charge dispersion over large molecular regions could be fine-tuned by the proper choice of cation [260].

Distinctive non-invasive diagnostics without expensive medical equipment may be one of the most prominent fields of medical diagnosis. Optical sensors, test equipment, and portable gadgets are the most suitable for such analyses. With the help of new sensory systems based on new chemical compounds that meet the requirements of "green chemistry," using the most recent advancements in materials science and microelectronics, the need to extend the range of decided compounds and objects of analysis determines the dynamic development of sensor technologies. According to recent literature, modern combinations that satisfy the requirements of "green chemistry" include ILs that can be employed as solvents in biosensor technologies, unique additions to polymer matrix transporters and their modifiers, and operate as plasticizers and identification agents.

In 2011, Jian-Hua Wang et al. [261] reported a symmetrical hydrophilic ILs BBIMCl, which generally shows weak fluorescence having QYs at enormously low levels of 0.005-0.02 in the non-aqueous medium concerning the conventional imidazolium ILs. Due to their planar conjugating structure and highly high QYs in an aqueous phase, these ILs exhibit excellent optical properties.
