Due to the coordination interactions between the iron atom in the hemoglobin's (Hb) heme group and the cationic imidazolium moiety, the presence of Hb could drastically reduce the fluorescence of BBIMCl, and it has the potential for the specific detection of Hb. However, a methylimidazolium chloride ILs-based sensor array confined in a PVC chain for in situ selective discrimination and quantification of Hb in the solid surface was reported by Wang and co-workers [262]. Schmitzer and Masson et al. [263] utilized the surface of imidazolium-based ILs for biosensing. ILs self-accumulated monolayers (SAM) were found to be beneficial for capturing streptavidin, promoting attraction biosensing and enzyme movement on the gold surfaces of the developed sensors. Streptavidin sensing with surface plasmon resonance (SPR) was made possible by the creation of biotinylated ILs, 1-((+)-biotin)pentanamido)propyl)-3-(12-mercaptododecyl)-imidazolium bromide. A carboxylated Ils, 1-(carboxymethyl)-3-(mercaptododecyl)-imidazolium bromide utilized to detect anti-immunoglobulin G in the nanomolar concentration range. By using [C8MIM][PF6], ILs as modifiers, Ghodratollah Absalan et al. [264] constructed an optical sensor for the quantification of ascorbic acid. Neocuproine was deposited on a transparent tri acetyl cellulose film as a membrane and used as an optode to detect ascorbic acid indirectly. Fast diffusion of analytes inside the bulk of the membrane was made possible by the ionic nature and liquid condition of ILs at room temperature, which may reduce the optode's reaction time. The minimal vapor pressure and chemical stability of ILs give the ILs-modified membrane an extended shelf life. Comparatively to solid materials, ILs could uniformly distribute across the membrane's surface, improving repeatability and the detection limit. The pH-independent optical detection of heparin based on ILs, 1-Dodecyl-3-methyl imidazolium chloride ([C12mim][Cl])-capped gold nanoparticles(AuNPs) was reported by Bahram Hemmateenejad and colleagues [265]. Based on an aggregation of gold nanoparticles (AuNPs), they offered a straightforward pH-independent optical approach for sensing the polyionic drug heparin, which is crucial for biomedicine. The surface plasmon band shift and a subsequent change in the color of the AuNPs from red to blue are caused by the aggregation of positively charged ILs stabilized AuNPs by polyanionic heparin. Increasing the added heparin concentration increases the degree of aggregation and allows its quantitative detection. Recently, an anion optode compromising TTPnFL ILs (n represent the length of alkyl chain in the FL moiety) as fluorescent moiety and plasticized PVC membrane reported by Hismoto co-workers to detect and quantify the heparin and observed to be 26 times higher sensitivity than the conventional optode made from FL and plasticized PVC membrane [266]. According to Bussamara and Scholten et al. [267], graphene oxide (GO) functionalized with amine-functionalized ILs (IL-1 & IL-2) and sputter-deposited gold nanoparticles (Au NPs) in combination with cholesterol oxidase appeared to be a suitable and effective biosensor for total cholesterol measurement (Fig. 19 ). When used in colorimetric testing, these biosensors demonstrated an excellent linear response (25-470 mol/L) for detecting total cholesterol. Control studies also indicated that total cholesterol concentration could be determined in the presence of interferents.

A low-cost paper strips-based test kit supporting with ILs containing 5-(dimethylamino)-N-methyl-N- (1-phenylpropan-2-yl)naphthalene-1-sulfonamide (FSP) as photoluminescence indicator fabricated by Deng co-workers [268] for on-site detection of methamphetamine (MA) drug in human urine which is generally used as a drug of abuse among young people [269]. In 2014, Galpothdeniya et al. [270] fabricated [P66614]2[FL], a luminous ILs sensor for the very accurate and specific detection of albumins. Weakly fluorescent monomeric forms turn to intensely luminous J-type aggregates in an aqueous medium due to the formation of nanodroplets. A remarkable structural change of albumin occurs in the presence of [P66614]2[FL], which was revealed by CD spectroscopic studies indicating strong interactions between ILs and albumins. When albumins are present, BSA and HSA transform [P66614]2[FL]'s aggregate forms into monomeric forms, which increases the photoluminescence signal proportionally. The alteration of [P66614]2[FL] J- aggregates into monomeric forms depends on the amounts of proteins in an aqueous medium. As a result, [P66614]2[FL] ought to be an excellent fluorescent probe for detecting HSA, specifically in watery samples. A three-component nano-GUMBOS, [P66614][RhB][FL], made from FL, RhB, and TTP, was demonstrated by Kelsey et al. in 2019 [271] with adjustments in pH. However, the size of the particles was much more significant (approx..70 nm) in very acidic or basic (pH 2 and 10, respectively) conditions. Nano[P66614][RhB][FL] emission spectra display two bands that correspond to the FL and RhB moieties. Except for pH 6, the luminosity rises at increasing pH levels due to increased anionic FL absorption and improved FL to RhB excitation energy transfer efficiency. Between pH 5 and pH 7, which is a crucial range for cellular pH sensing, the excitation energy transfer efficiency ratio varied dramatically, indicating its potential as a diagnostic tool to identify cancer cells. The selective distribution of fluorescence in cancer cells was observed to be higher than in normal cells when treated with [P66614][RhB][FL] nanoparticles under comparable experimental conditions. With equally evaluated parent chemicals, such discrimination was not possible. This visual difference indicates more significant potential for the use of [P66614][RhB][FL] nanoGUMBOS in the optical detection of cancer. A 6-(p-toluidino)-2- naphthalenesulfonate (TNS)-based sensor array based on GUMBOS was introduced for sensitive and label-free sensing of proteins [272]. Recently, a series of TC-based nano-GUMBOS developed to discriminate proteins array with high accuracy has excellent potential for sensitive and precise medical diagnosis and discrimination of other biomolecules [273]. Further, erythrosin B-based GUMBOS and nanoGUMBOS were also developed for discrimination and quantification of protein over the other biomolecules [274,275]

An enzyme-responsive optode comprising PVC as a plasticizer and a coumarin-based (12-PC) photoluminescence ILs as a signaling unit was demonstrated by Hisamoto co-workers [276] for accurate and sensitive detection of alkaline phosphatase (ALP) and found to be six times higher sensitive than the conventional membrane employed for the protein discrimination. Further, the same ILs were emulsified at the nano level using pluronic F-127 as an emulsifier and utilized for the detection and quantification of ALP. However, the response time was reduced by half compared to the previous one [277]. The 12-PC-based nanoemulsion preparation process and ALP detection mechanism are demonstrated in Fig. 20 .

In this review, we have tried to elaborate on the ILs assimilated sustainable advanced functional materials where ILs have been used as a building block or components for versatile optoelectronics applications. ILs are a revolutionary platform for manufacturing sophisticated functional photonic materials due to their ability to concurrently achieve high thermal stability, low cost, and environmental friendliness. These ILs-based photonic materials' optimization and realization allow enhanced optoelectronic applications with improved performance, which helps to realize its potentiality in various optoelectronic applications. ILs-integrated advanced photonic materials could be classified into three types: (1) task-specific or functional ILs, which are liquidized ionic materials that could be designed with integration of proper functionalization for desired applications, (2) materials that have been chemically modified using ion-exchange metathesis or covalent functionalization to produce ILs-amalgamated materials, (3) ILs-based composite materials are created by physically combining ILs with conventional materials, respectively. Depending on the variation of ionic constituents such as cationic and anionic alkyl chain length and incorporation of functional groups in the cationic and anionic moieties, the thermophysical and other properties of these IL-based photofunctional materials could be fine-tuned, and the advanced functional materials could be easily fabricated as mono to multifunctional materials for the desired applications. As an illustration, PILs are considered a brand-new functional material that combines the qualities of both ILs and polymers. The features of ILs can be combined in metal-fused ILs depending on the composite metal ions; therefore, they can once again be considered multifunctional materials.

Undoubtedly, the permutation and combination of ILs chemistry with material science will form more and more new innovative photonic materials with better properties and surprising performance compared to traditional ones. However, the use of ILs-based photonic materials for technological and industrial applications is still in its infancy. This is because the cost of many ILs is exceptionally high, which is unacceptable for practical applications, and the commercial availability of ILs is very constrained to small volumes. Once more, the process of purifying ILs is exceedingly time-consuming. Small levels of contaminants can greatly impact the physicochemical and photophysical characteristics of ILs. However, oxygen-scavenging of optical materials by oxygen poses a severe problem for using ILs in various optoelectronics technologies. The fluidity of ILs can occasionally make it difficult for optoelectronic devices to be packaged for long-term use in practical applications. In contrast to conventional molecular materials, these ionic materials coexist with various interactions that allow for the development of more sophisticated photonic materials. To realize these innovative photonic materials' full potential, however, a careful balance of these interactions and a fundamental comprehension of the structure-properties connection and performance of these materials are needed. For example, introducing a bulky functional group, ionic species, or long alkyl chain length in the ionic components could significantly depress the melting points of ionic materials, favoring the formation of liquidized ionic materials. Ionic counter ions should be varied to fine-tune the physicochemical properties. Instead of many challenges, progress on ILs-based photonic materials has been made significantly in recent years. The materialization of ILs created an opportunity to develop new photonic advanced functional materials by tuning the physicochemical properties of traditional materials and opened a new avenue for intensive ILs research, which will bridge inorganic, organic, molecular, ionic, solid, and liquid materials to make hybrid materials for promising optoelectronic applications.
